title: Best Buy
description: Compare unit rates in everyday scenarios.

miniLesson.title: Finding a Unit Rate
miniLesson.paragraph: A **unit rate** tells you how much of one thing you get for a single unit of another, like dollars per pound or miles per hour. To find it, **divide** the total by the quantity: $\text{unit rate} = \frac{\text{total}}{\text{quantity}}$. Keep the order straight - price per pound is dollars $\div$ pounds, not the other way around.

example.prompt: **4 oranges** cost **$2**. What is the price per orange?

miniLesson.title: Comparing Two Deals
miniLesson.paragraph: To find the **better deal**, you can't just compare totals - the sizes differ. Instead, reduce each option to its **unit price** by dividing price by quantity, then pick the **lower** one. A bigger package is only a better deal if its price *per unit* is smaller.

example.prompt: Bag A: **2 lb for $4**. Bag B: **3 lb for $4.50**. Which is the better deal per pound?

options.label: Bag A ($2.00/lb)
options.whyWrong: Bag A is more per pound - 4 ÷ 2 = $2.00 beats 4.50 ÷ 3 = $1.50.

options.label: Same price per lb
options.whyWrong: The unit prices differ - $2.00 vs $1.50 per pound.

example.explanation: Bag B is $4.50 \div 3 = \$1.50$ per pound versus $4 \div 2 = \$2.00$ for Bag A, so B is cheaper per pound.

miniLesson.title: Using a Rate
miniLesson.paragraph: Once you know a rate, you can scale it up. To find a **total cost**, **multiply** the price per item by how many you buy: $\text{total} = \text{rate} \times \text{quantity}$. To find **how many you can afford**, do the reverse and **divide** your budget by the price.

example.prompt: Pencils cost **$0.50** each. How much do **6 pencils** cost?

miniLesson.title: Fixed Fee Plus Rate
miniLesson.paragraph: Many real-world prices have **two parts**: a one-time fixed fee plus a rate that grows with use. The pattern is $\text{total} = \text{base fee} + \text{rate} \times \text{quantity}$. The most common mistake is forgetting the base fee, so always add it back in at the end.

example.prompt: A delivery costs **$5** plus **$2** per package. What is the cost for **3 packages**?

miniLesson.title: Comparing Whole Plans
miniLesson.paragraph: Now combine every skill: compute each plan's **full cost** before comparing them. A flat plan stays the same, while a two-part plan uses $\text{base} + \text{rate} \times \text{quantity}$. Make sure both costs cover the **same amount of usage** (and the same time period) before deciding which is cheaper.

example.prompt: Plan A: **$20 flat**. Plan B: **$5 plus $1 per GB**. For **10 GB**, which plan is cheaper?

options.label: Plan A ($20)
options.whyWrong: Plan B totals only $15 at 10 GB, which is less than Plan A's $20.

options.label: Same cost
options.whyWrong: The totals differ - $20 versus $15 for 10 GB.

example.explanation: Plan B costs $5 + 1 \times 10 = \$15$, which is less than Plan A's flat $20.
